text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic appendicitomy. This patient has acute appendicitis. She is a female and ultrasound and CT scan both has Confirm the edematous inflamed appendix. Total count was also raised and she has a severe pain in right iliac fossa together with the rebound tenderness, Robson's sign. So it is a confirmed case of acute appendicitis.

The surgeon introduces a Veress needle at the inferior crease of the umbilicus, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests. Tubing is attached to the insufflator, and pneumoperitoneum is initiated with a preset pressure of fifteen. The actual pressure gradually increases, with the flow rate initially at one liter per minute, then increased to three liters per minute. The incision is enlarged to eleven millimeters.

A smiling incision is made over the crease of the umbilicus, and the optical trocar is introduced with a hissing sound. The telescope provides a view of the appendix, showing a healthy base but an inflamed and adhered tip. The appendix is retrocecal and subserous, adhered to the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the inflamed and adhered appendix tip from the small intestine and surrounding tissue. The appendix is prepared for anti-grade appendectomy using extracorporeal Misra's knot due to its impending rupture.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum covering part of the appendix using a Striker mini alligator instrument to examine the base of the appendix. Further peritoneal dissection is performed to mobilize the appendix, revealing the inflamed tip adhered to the cecum. An extracorporeal knot is prepared to secure the structure, starting from the base to avoid perforation while separating the tip.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the appendix and creates a window at the base using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the Teflon jaw only touches the cecum. A small buttonhole is carefully made at the base of the appendix to avoid injury to the cecum. Blunt dissection is used to open the window, and a Vicryl suture is fed through the buttonhole. The surgeon ties an extracorporeal Misra's knot with a single hitch, three winds, and three locks, using a knot pusher to secure it at the base of the appendix, effectively ligating it.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the appendix, leaving a five-millimeter margin towards the cecum. The energy from the scalpel seals the distal end, preventing fecolith slippage. The base of the appendix is separated and ligated, allowing for the gradual dissection of the appendix from the subserous area of the cecum. The appendix, which is retrocecal and adhered, is dissected towards the tip using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, with anterior traction applied by a grasper.

The surgeon carefully maintains a safe distance from the cecum while using a stripping technique to separate the inflamed and adhered appendix. The harmonic device is used to coagulate and strip down the appendix, reaching the tip, which is adhered and surrounded by resistant fat. The surgeon sacrifices a small portion of the serosa at the tip using the harmonic to avoid perforating the ascending colon or cecum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the appendix from the ascending colon and cecum, ensuring the Teflon jaw is positioned towards the cecum for safety. The appendix is fully detached and then pulled into a cannula using a cloth grasper. An endo bag may be used, but the appendix is brought into the cannula and removed. Betadine irrigation is performed, followed by suction to remove the irritant. The surgeon conducts a flat tire test by tilting the patient to the right, irrigating with saline, and pressing the cecum and ascending colon to check for bubbles, indicating potential perforation.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the surgical area, ensuring all betadine is removed. The absence of bubbles during the saline test confirms the integrity of the cecum, indicating no perforation.
